speaker: interviewee
text: [BACKGROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewee
text: Huh? [INTERPOSING VOICES]

speaker: interviewer
text: Good timing.

speaker: interviewee
text: Robert.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the clock-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] That's another thing. I know. Yeah. In the middle. Maybe we could--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you think that you-- are you-- are you on headset? Do you think we picked that clock up? [BACKGROUND NOISE] We did?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Can-- Let's see. Rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape three, interview with Gloria Ungar, January 18, 1995 in Los Angeles. You were just starting to tell us about the kapos. Tell me what you remember about the kapos.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember the kapos, that they were very, very tough with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: These were Jewish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish women, yes. They were women who were there already a long time. And they had to fend for themselves in order to survive. And they were very tough.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what way were they tough? What did they used to do?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were very strict.

speaker: interviewer
text: What would--

speaker: interviewee
text: They had-- you know, they had like, their own room in the-- in the barrack. They had their own room. There were usually two kapos to a barrack. And they got better food. They had, I guess, better connections where to get all these things.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they were not very nice, you know? If you approached them, you asked for something, or-- you know, they could slap you, or beat you, or whatever, you know, without mercy. [BACKGROUND NOISE] They, they had no pity on anybody. Were you afraid of them?

speaker: interviewee
text: I stayed away, you know? I didn't look for problems, you know? I was always trying to survive and not to cause any problems.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm. Did you-- did you think of them as being allied with the Germans? Or did you recognize then that, that-- that they didn't probably have a lot of options?

speaker: interviewee
text: I, I recognized that they don't have any options, you know. It was just-- they just wanted to save themselves. That's all. Mhm. Were they hated by the other women by then? Yes, they were.

speaker: interviewer
text: They were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, they were. I mean, you know, they'd been there-- some of them were there for three years or four years. And they just developed a very thick hide, you know? They saw what was going on and they knew that they probably-- maybe they won't survive either, but that they have to try.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they travel with you when you'd go from camp to camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. They stayed behind. They stayed behind, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm. And organized the next group in?

speaker: interviewee
text: I guess so. I guess so. As we stepped out from the barracks, the electric barbed wire was right there, you know. So, you know, there was nothing to do-- just to step to the wire and get killed, you know. Either you went to work or your other alternative was to just go get yourself killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the kapos, they, they kept order? They kept--

speaker: interviewee
text: They kept order. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they-- were they involved with serving the food--

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Providing the food?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. No. But they, they were managing it, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: Managing the distribution of--

speaker: interviewee
text: The distribution, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember how they would slice the bread and, and things like that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was a piece of bread like that. And you know, you had to decide for yourself what you're going to do. Are you going to eat it up all now, or are you going to save a piece for later? You know?

speaker: interviewer
text: So at first, I tried to eat half of the piece and save a piece for the night. [BACKGROUND NOISE] But somebody got to my piece, and they, they stole it. And from then on, I just ate it up, and that's all. Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I remember somebody on the train-- somebody grabbed for my piece of bread. And I still have here two marks from their nails that they grabbed my piece of bread.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hm. [SIGHS] So four, four camps in how long? Can you remember? Any idea?

speaker: interviewee
text: From September. And we were liberated May 4th. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went first to-- you were in Auschwitz, then Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewee
text: And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Then the work camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Magdeburg.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then four more camps from there?

speaker: interviewee
text: And four more camps. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they kept you marching, and they would put you in trains.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. On our last trip in the train, we travelled for-- maybe for two weeks in the trains without water, without food. Occasionally they would stop the train and bring some water or distribute some food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they let you off at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'll get to that, when they let us off. And we were packed like sardines. And I just-- [SIGHS] I-- I just kept on telling myself, remember who you are, because I just felt like I'm losing my mind. And I'm-- I just kept on telling myself, remember, your name is Gitcha Nagel. You were born September 9, 1930. Don't forget who you are. Just keep on-- keep on remembering that, who you are, because after a while you just even don't feel like a human being anymore. And my-- evidently my lungs were compressed a while, because I couldn't talk anymore. I was so weak. And I, I couldn't utter a word. No sound would come from my, my-- from my mouth. And they stopped the trains. We were in a forest. And they stopped the trains to unload the dead people, just to shove them off. And they shoved, shoved off the dead ones. And they shoved me off, too. I was so skeletal. I probably must have weighed maybe 30 pounds, maybe 35 pounds. And they shoved me off of the train.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I just fought like a tiger to get back on the train, you know? I just scrambled with all my energy to get back on the train, because I knew if I'm left behind there, I'll be lying dead like-- just like all the others there. And I made it up on the train.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I think two days later, we arrived to Denmark. They opened up the doors, and we are in Denmark. It seems that it was already end of the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Count Bernadotte, I think he was Swedish or Danish. I'm not sure. I think he was-- he was a Swede. He offered the Germans to give them supplies if he would turn over some of these inmates to Denmark. And I guess that's where they brought us, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: They opened up the trains. And there were Danish people there with baskets of foods. And they opened up, and they said, you are free. You are free. Well, many people jumped off. The ones that had the energy jumped off, and they ran for the food. And some of them died afterwards because their stomach couldn't take it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I was just sitting in the train. Remember, I was just sitting-- me and a few other people-- like in a daze. And thinking to myself, so what? So it's over. Well, what does it matter? I mean, I'm left alone in this world. I, I, I don't-- I don't even know who I am anymore. I have nobody. I have no home. I have no family. I-- it just-- It just didn't mean anything to me, you know? I just couldn't be happy.

speaker: interviewee
text: But they took us off the trains. And the, the Danish people were just wonderful. Just wonderful. They, they welcomed us there. And they put us in quarantine. And they found out that I have tuberculosis. I was running a very high fever. And they gave me anesthetic, which had-- which I didn't know at that time what it was. I was a young child. I didn't know what it was. They cut open my wounds to drain the pus from the vitamin deficiency, all the pus-- all the wounds that I had. And I was-- just remember one thing. As they put the mask on my face to give me the anesthetic, I was thinking, oh my god. Now I'm dying. Now I'm going to die, and nobody will even know that I survived the war. But I survived that. And after that, they took us by boat to Sweden. And we were put in the hospitals. And there they treated us wonderfully. The Swedish people were just-- I mean, so different from the Polish people and from the Slovakians.

speaker: interviewer
text: It's just hard to believe that, that different people can be different, you know? They were just so wonderful. As a matter of fact, there was one family who came to visit me in the hospital, and they wanted to adopt me.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I said no. I hope to find somebody. And I hope to find-- to find something. And I did. My father-- my brother one day-- he was in Prague-- and he read in the paper that I am a survivor and I am in Sweden.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that-- how did that get into the paper?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were publishing, I guess, names of survivors. And he just read about me. And he got in touch with me. And I was already at that time-- after I got better, after I was not contagious anymore, they put me into a school which was just for children, for, for surviving children. And they had-- they had teachers that came from Palestine to teach these children and to take them on the illegal alias.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when my, my brother found out that I am there, he managed to come to Sweden on a bomber. At that time, it was very hard to come on a plane, you know? They didn't have commercial planes, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: He came to see-- to see me. And he persuaded me not to go to Palestine, because he says, you've been already in enough camps. And let's not go to Palestine. And then we will be caught on the boats and taken to Cyprus. And in Cyprus, we would have to be in camps again. And we just didn't want to see another barbed wire again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you remember when you saw your brother for the first time?

speaker: interviewee
text: [SIGHS] Ah. I Remember so vividly. I remember I was already at school at that time. And we were up on a hill. The school was up on a hill. [SIGHS] And when I saw him coming, I just ran down the hill. And we hugged and we kissed. And I just-- I'll never forget that. All my friends were so jealous that I had a brother. I'll never forget that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he look all right? Had he--

speaker: interviewee
text: He looked all right. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: He looked all right.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were now 15 years old?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how old would he have been?

speaker: interviewee
text: Probably 22 maybe.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: And it was just the two of you left?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just the two of us left. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he know about what had happened with your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we have-- we have a friend living here in Los Angeles. His name is Rabbi Rubin. And he was with my father in Bergen-Belsen. And he said he saw my father two days before the liberation yet. And he thinks that my father and another friend of his was taken and shot. They were shot two days before the liberation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where had your brother been?

speaker: interviewee
text: My brother has been to Auschwitz and a few other camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't exactly know where.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he went home afterward-- after liberation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. He went to Slovakia back.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. He didn't go back to Krosno?

speaker: interviewee
text: To where?

speaker: interviewer
text: To Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no. That was our first trip. Two years ago, we went back on our first trip. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why didn't he-- why didn't you consider going back home?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Poland? There was nothing there for us. We had nobody there. Nobody survived from our Polish relatives. So we didn't want to go back. And we had very bad memories of Poland. The people were very, very anti-Semitic and very bad. And they, they just collaborated with the Germans. They just wanted us to-- they just wanted us out of the way.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Even now when I went back to Poland for the visit. We went to see our home, and the lady was so nasty. I asked her, could you-- would you let us in? I was born here. I would like to see-- she says, oh, I had a dream. I knew that they're going to come and take back our places. I said, I didn't come back to take the property. I said, you can keep the property. All I want to do is just, just show my-- I had my nieces and nephews with us to show them where we lived.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had very bad memories of the Polish people. That doesn't mean that there weren't any good ones. I'm sure there were good ones, too. But the ones that I remember, they were all very bad to us. And what about Slovakia? Did-- you didn't--

speaker: interviewee
text: Slovakia was less anti-Semitic, but also not--

speaker: interviewer
text: But you didn't want to go there?

speaker: interviewee
text: But they were also very happy to get rid of the Jews, because they could take their properties and you know, they could take whatever they want, whatever the Jews had.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you had made a plan with your aunt to meet her.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, after the war, before I came to the United States, I went back to Czechoslovakia. She survived with her little son. But unfortunately, he died right after that.

speaker: interviewer
text: He died--

speaker: interviewee
text: He got also tuberculosis, and he didn't make it. He did not survive. He died right after.

speaker: interviewer
text: So your brother came to Sweden to see you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. And he went back to Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: The two of you went back to--

speaker: interviewee
text: No, only, only him. I stayed in Sweden because I went to school there. And I had no reason to go back. I had nobody there. I had no mother, no father, no, no home, no nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Didn't you want to stay with your brother?

speaker: interviewee
text: I did, but he was a single fellow. And I mean, what, what could we, the two of us, do? Here I was taken care of. I was being educated, and fed, and taken care of.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I stayed in Sweden, and he went back to Czechoslovakia. And he tried to get papers for us to come to the United States. And he promised me-- he says, we'll come to the United States. And if you will decide there that you don't want to stay there and you want to go to Palestine, I will not be in your way. Then we will both go to Palestine. But, of course, we came to the United States, and we stayed here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me about where you lived in Sweden. Did you live with a family?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. It was-- it was a home for children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: For surviving children.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was all-- you're Eastern European children?

speaker: interviewee
text: All East European children. There were girls from-- mainly from Slovakia and Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you stay there?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was there for two years. One year I was in the hospital, and one year I was at school.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you didn't know for more than a year that your brother was still alive?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe, maybe-- I don't know whether it was a year-- maybe 10 months or eight months. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: That you found out?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. That-- yeah. He found out that I am alive.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know that-- you described that moment of reunion. Did you know that you would be seeing him that day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I, I think so. He told me when, when-- that he's arriving to Stockholm. And from there, he will take a train. And probably there-- and-- as a matter of fact, no. I did not know that he was coming. A friend of mine came running to tell me that she thinks my brother is coming. And as I was standing up the hill and looking down, I recognized him right away. And I was just running like crazy.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you're-- now you're waiting to get your papers to come to the United States.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you have to wait?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came to the United States, I believe, in '49.

speaker: interviewer
text: With your brother.

speaker: interviewee
text: Till '47, I stayed in Sweden. And-- no, in '47. I was three years in Sweden, and then we got the papers to come to the United States. And I came separately from Sweden, and my brother followed me from Czechoslovakia.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you, you came to the United States-- you, you travelled to the United States alone?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Again?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you travel to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: By boat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: I came on the boat [NON-ENGLISH], a Swedish boat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had a single uncle here already, so he was waiting for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where?

speaker: interviewee
text: He lived in the Bronx. He was single. And he came a couple of months before I did. And he came to the boat, and he waited for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he took me by the hand, and he was very protective of me. He wouldn't let me cross the street alone. And I said, please, Uncle Simon, what are you doing? I said, I-- I was all over by myself. You think I wouldn't know how to cross the street in the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] He was a wonderful uncle.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what, what the United States seemed like to you when you got here? You're now 17.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very exciting. Very exciting. I was-- [SIGHS] I was-- it was really wonderful. I celebrated my-- the first year I was here, I celebrated my 16th birthday.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your 16th or 17th?

speaker: interviewee
text: 16Th.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there-- so you were born in '30, so it was only '46 when you got here.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in September, I became-- I came in May.

speaker: interviewer
text: Uh huh.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in September I would have been 16.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. So you remember having your birthday?

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember my first 16th. And of course, here in the United States, sweet 16 is a big deal here. But, anyhow, I, I celebrated with my brother and some of my friends. Yeah. I came here, and, of course, I had no family again. So my single uncle couldn't take care of me. So they decided that they'll put me-- it was-- I came in May. And I lived with a family in the Bronx. We paid them, and they-- you know, I lived with them. I had a room with them. And when it was time to-- for the summer, they put me to a camp. They put me in a children's camp. And there I learned enough English to be able to go to school in September.

speaker: interviewer
text: To go to high school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to high school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Hm. Was it a camp-- a summer camp, or was it a camp to teach you English?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. It was a summer camp. It was a summer camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: I came--

speaker: interviewer
text: And this is still in New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: I came on the papers. I came on a student visa. And the student visa was from Bais Yaakov, so I went to their camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: I see. So this was in-- still in New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was 1947. Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: In New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you-- so you went to Bais Yaakov, and you lived with a family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you talk about that you paid to do-- to stay there and everything. Where did-- where did your family get money? You must have been very destitute.

speaker: interviewee
text: Where did we get money? I don't know. My-- then, after-- I guess they helped us to put me in camp, you know? I imagine that they didn't have to pay much for me, you know? But after that, I started school. After the summer, I started school. We took a little apartment, my brother, myself, and my uncle, my single uncle. And I was the housekeeper. [LAUGHS] And I went to school. Yeah. And they went to work.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm-hm. And this is still in New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: And they supported us. That was in New York. And after my aunt came-- this aunt who survived, you know, this Aunt Mina, who was a wonderful lady. And she took me in. And I lived with them for a while while I went to school. And I lived with them. By then, she had two other children. She had-- her husband came back. He lives here in Los Angeles. She passed on already, but her husband lives here.

speaker: interviewer
text: She had two children who had survived, or that she had after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Afterwards, she had another-- this child didn't survive, but she had three other children. At that time, she had only one. And they came to the United States. And they took me in, and I lived for awhile with them. And I went to school. And I went to Hunter College for two years. And then I decided I have to make a living.

speaker: interviewer
text: [LAUGHS] Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I, I got a job at the Government of Israel Supply Mission, which was-- it was-- at that time already, there was, there was already Israel, you know? So they, they needed supplies for the state. And it was-- it was a wonderful job. I loved it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm. So how did you meet your husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: And how did I meet my husband? Through a cousin of ours. My cousin, her name was [NON-ENGLISH]. She was a very good, god friend of my husband's family in Europe. And one day they got together with my husband's-- Victor's-- cousin in Brooklyn. And they said, I have the two most wonderful children. They have to meet.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: And Victor called me. He wanted to take me out. I said, no. I don't think I'm going to go out with him, you know? Then he called again. I said, well, OK, I'm going to go out with him. And then when I met him, I, I knew for sure that I'm going to marry him. [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewer
text: I knew he was the person for me. So did you marry in New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We met in New York. And then after awhile, we moved to California, because Victor had one surviving brother who lived in Chicago. And he decided to move to California. And my brother also came to California, and he met a local girl and he married her. So there was no reason for us to stay in New York. So we moved to California.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you started a family.

speaker: interviewee
text: And here we, we-- here we started a family. Actually, our oldest son was born still in New York. Robert was born in New York. And then we had Jeffrey and Michael. They were born here.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to talk to you, when you came, about being religious.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because you came to New York, and Bais Yaakov--

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Put you in school.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: After all that you had gone through, and-- why did you still have the feeling that you wanted to observe the tradition?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, honestly speaking, I was not religious after the war. I just-- I mean, I had my upbringing, my religious upbringing, and you just can't shed that, you know? It's with you. And I always had that religious feeling, you know?

speaker: interviewer
text: But it wasn't like before the war, you know? I mean, we were not so religious. Everything was permissible already. We felt that we went through so much that it's OK to do it. It's all right. It's OK. And we were not so religious. I was not so religious.

speaker: interviewee
text: But our children brought us back to religion. They wanted to be religious, our children. They just-- we sent them to Hebrew schools, and, and they just felt because of our suffering that they just wanted to be religious.

speaker: interviewer
text: We need to say your husband was a survivor as well.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a survivor. Yeah. He was not in a concentration camp, but he was a survivor.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was hiding. He lived on-- he was blonde and he didn't look like a Jew, so he, he was-- he had papers, Christian papers. And he survived in Paris with his brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Gloria, how do you think you did survive? What, what was there that helped you to keep going?

speaker: interviewee
text: I really don't know. Maybe it's because I was young and I was hopeful. And when you are young, you-- you always think that-- you want to hold on. You always think that things will be OK. Just hold on, there. Maybe it will be OK. I even remember when we were on our way to Auschwitz and we knew for sure that, that we can't survive, I kept on thinking to myself-- it's funny that you should remember these things. I mean, you know, it was 50 years ago. That I should remember-- I was thinking, well, maybe, maybe somebody will have pity on me, or maybe something will happen that I will be not sent to the gas chamber.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then that woman came and took you out of line.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then that woman came. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were alone. It was a little unusual to be that alone. Usually there's somebody from the town or something.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember feeling more vulnerable because you were so alone?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, absolutely. That lady who brought me that piece of bread in Bergen-Belsen, her name is Figa Parnis. She lives now in New York. And she took me under her wing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewee
text: She said, you are going to be my lager sister. And I take you under my wing and-- wings. And she was really very helpful.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she your age or a little older?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, she was older.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she an adult?

speaker: interviewee
text: She was?

speaker: interviewer
text: About how old was she? She wasn't old enough to be your--

speaker: interviewee
text: She must have been 18.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, she wasn't old enough to be your mother or anything like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no, no. No, she must have been 18, 19.

speaker: interviewer
text: But she befriended you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, she just befriended me because she knew my family from home. She knew my aunt's family from home.

speaker: interviewer
text: From Slovakia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. That's how she knew your-- that that was your aunt.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Right. Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had her.

speaker: interviewee
text: I had her for a while, and then we also got separated. I don't know how or what. You know, they didn't ask you. They-- you know, they counted the people. Now you go here. And the other people went there. And you had no control over that-- with whom you stayed or with whom you went, with whom you went.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you think about it now, do you think that you had more trouble making a-- having relationships with friends and family because you were so alone and had been so isolated? [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I had no problem making friends. I was always a sweet child. I was jealous of the other people who had somebody, you know? Where there were two sisters together or two cousins together, I, I was jealous. I wanted to belong to somebody. But I didn't have anybody, you know? Everybody was, was for themselves.

speaker: interviewer
text: But now you have a lot of people.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now I have a lovely, lovely family. I have a wonderful, wonderful husband. We've been married for 42 years. I hope for many, many more. And we have just the three most wonderful sons. Really, the best there are. And they married wonderful, wonderful girls. They are like really our daughters. And between them, we have the most beautiful 12 grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, maybe we can meet some of your family.

speaker: interviewee
text: I hope so.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
